---
title: "01 - Maximal Returns Protocol: Mixed-Effects Analysis"
author: "Dr. Jacob Bowie"
date: "`r format(Sys.Date(), '%B %d, %Y')`"
output:
  html_document:
    toc: true
    toc_float: true
    toc_depth: 3
    code_folding: hide
    fig_width: 10
    fig_height: 6
    dpi: 300
params:
  show_all_plots: FALSE
  save_pdfs: FALSE
  cache: FALSE
---

# Maximal returns protocol (MRP) analysis

### Environment setup

```{r setup, echo=FALSE, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE, cache=FALSE}
source("helpers/setup.R")
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  cache = params$cache,
  echo = FALSE,
  message = FALSE,
  warning = FALSE
)
SHOW_ALL_PLOTS <- isTRUE(params$show_all_plots)
SAVE_PDFS      <- isTRUE(params$save_pdfs)

# Legacy compatibility: downstream chunks reference output_directory and date
output_directory <- output_dir
date <- format(Sys.Date(), "%m%d%Y")
```

```{r load-data, include=FALSE}
# Path to data file (absolute, invariant under knitr chunk-WD restoration)
data_path <- file.path(DATA_DIR, "MRP.xlsx")

# Exercise name standardization
replacements <- c(
  "Overhead Press" = "OHP",
  "Lat Pull down" = "Lat Pull",
  "Leg Press"     = "Leg Press",
  "Bent Over Row" = "Bent Row",
  "Seated Row"    = "Seated Row",
  "Lat Pulldown"  = "Lat Pull"
)

# Exercise groupings
upper_exercises <- c("OHP", "Lat Pull", "Bent Row", "Seated Row")
lower_exercises <- c("Leg Press", "Deadlift")

# Subject classification
trained_subjects   <- paste0("SUB ", c(8, 17, 18, 19))
untrained_subjects <- paste0("SUB ", c(2, 3, 10, 11, 14))

# Sex mapping
sex_mapping <- data.frame(
  Subject = c("SUB 2", "SUB 3", "SUB 8", "SUB 10", "SUB 11",
              "SUB 14", "SUB 17", "SUB 18", "SUB 19"),
  Sex = c("Female", "Male", "Male", "Male", "Female",
          "Male", "Male", "Male", "Female")
)

# 1. STRENGTH DATA PROCESSING
strength_data <- read_excel(data_path, sheet = "Strength", col_names = TRUE) %>%
  dplyr::select(1:3, 7:9) %>%
  rename(Subject = 1, Measure = 2, Exercise = 3, PRE = 4, MID = 5, POST = 6) %>%
  filter(!(Subject %in% c("SUB 5", "SUB 13"))) %>%
  mutate(
    PRE = as.numeric(PRE),
    MID = as.numeric(MID),
    POST = as.numeric(POST)
  ) %>%
  mutate(Exercise = dplyr::recode(Exercise, !!!replacements)) %>%
  add_training_status(trained = trained_subjects, untrained = untrained_subjects) %>%
  mutate(Measure_Exercise = paste(Measure, Exercise, sep = "_")) %>%
  filter(Measure_Exercise != "1RM_Bent Row" & Measure_Exercise != "10RM_Bent Row") %>%
  # Fix duplicated SUB 3 Seated Row entry
  filter(!(Subject == "SUB 3" &
           Measure == "10RM" &
           Exercise == "Seated Row" &
           PRE == 90.7 & MID == 93.0 & POST == 95.3)) %>%
  mutate(DataType = "Strength")

# 2. VO2 DATA PROCESSING
vo2_data <- read_excel(data_path, sheet = "vo2data", col_names = TRUE) %>%
  add_training_status(trained = trained_subjects, untrained = untrained_subjects) %>%
  mutate(
    Measure_Exercise = Measure,
    Exercise = Measure,
    DataType = "Physiological"
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    PRE = as.numeric(PRE),
    MID = as.numeric(MID),
    POST = as.numeric(POST)
  )

# 3. COMBINE DATASETS
mydata_combined <- bind_rows(
  strength_data %>% dplyr::select(Subject, Measure, Exercise, PRE, MID, POST,
                                  TrainingStatus, Measure_Exercise, DataType),
  vo2_data %>% dplyr::select(Subject, Measure, Exercise, PRE, MID, POST,
                              TrainingStatus, Measure_Exercise, DataType)
)

# Legacy compatibility: format_pvalue (used downstream until Phase 3 replaces with fmt_p)
format_pvalue <- function(p) {
  if (is.na(p)) return("N/A")
  if (p < 0.001) return("<0.001***")
  if (p < 0.01) return(paste0(round(p, 3), "**"))
  if (p < 0.05) return(paste0(round(p, 3), "*"))
  return(as.character(round(p, 3)))
}

```

## Assessing Normality and homogeneity of variance

```{r Assessing Normality and homogeneity of variance, echo=FALSE, results='asis'}
# Pivot the data into a longer format
mydata_long <- pivot_longer(mydata_combined, cols = c(PRE, MID, POST),
                            names_to = "Time", values_to = "Value")

# Group  data by 'Measure_Exercise' and perform the Shapiro-Wilk test for each group
normality_tests <- mydata_long %>%
  group_by(Measure_Exercise) %>%
  summarize(p_value = shapiro.test(Value)$p.value, .groups = 'drop')
# Print the results
cat(as.character(
  create_styled_kable(normality_tests,
    caption = "Shapiro-Wilk test for normality by Measure_Exercise",
    digits = 3)
))

# Create a histogram facetted by "Measure_Exercise" and colored by Time
ggplot(mydata_long, aes(x = Value, fill = Time)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 2, position = "dodge") +
  facet_wrap(~ Measure_Exercise, scales = "free") +  # Updated to allow for free scales if necessary
  labs(x = "Value", y = "Frequency") +
  ggtitle("Histogram Facetted by Measure_Exercise and Colored by Time") +
  theme_pubr()
```
### Overall Normality Assessment

Initial Shapiro-Wilk testing grouped by measure revealed that most variables were normally distributed, with several exceptions primarily among physiological measures. Strength measures generally met normality assumptions, while running economy measures (6.5 km·hr⁻¹, 12 km·hr⁻¹), aerobic capacity measures (VO₂Max, VO₂Peak), and functional performance measures (YYIR) showed significant departures from normality (*p* < 0.05).

```{r Assessing Normality and homogeneity of variance2, echo=FALSE, results='asis'}
# Test normality for each Measure_Exercise by TrainingStatus
normality_tests <- mydata_long %>%
  group_by(Measure_Exercise, TrainingStatus) %>%
  summarize(p_value = shapiro.test(Value)$p.value, .groups = 'drop')

# Filter and display significant non-normal results
sig_non_normal <- normality_tests %>% 
  filter(p_value < 0.05)

# Print the results
cat(as.character(
  create_styled_kable(sig_non_normal,
    caption = "Shapiro-Wilk: non-normal distributions by Measure x TrainingStatus",
    digits = 3)
))

# Create histogram only if there are significant non-normal measures
if (nrow(sig_non_normal) > 0) {
  # Get the unique combinations for filtering
  non_normal_combinations <- sig_non_normal %>%
    select(Measure_Exercise)
  
  # Filter data for plotting - only the significant non-normal combinations
  mydata_filtered <- mydata_long %>%
    semi_join(non_normal_combinations, by = c("Measure_Exercise"))
  
  # Create the plot
  plot_non_normal <- ggplot(mydata_filtered, aes(x = Value, fill = Time)) +
    geom_histogram(binwidth = 2, position = "dodge", alpha = 0.7) +
    facet_grid(TrainingStatus ~ Measure_Exercise, scales = "free") +
    ggtitle("Histogram of significantly non-normal measures by training status") +
    theme_pubr() +
    theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))
  
  print(plot_non_normal)
  
} else {
  cat("No significantly non-normal distributions found (p < 0.05)")
}
```



```{r Assessing Normality and homogeneity of variance 3, echo=FALSE, warning=FALSE, results='asis'}
# Group data by 'Measure_Exercise' and 'Time' perform the Shapiro-Wilk test for each group
normality_tests <- mydata_long %>%
  group_by(Measure_Exercise, Time) %>%
  summarize(p_value = shapiro.test(Value)$p.value, .groups = 'drop')
# Filter for sig non-normality 
filtered_normality_tests <- filter(normality_tests, p_value < 0.05)
cat(as.character(
  create_styled_kable(filtered_normality_tests,
    caption = "Shapiro-Wilk: non-normal by Measure x Timepoint (OHP, Seated Row)",
    digits = 3)
))
```



```{r Assessing Normality and homogeneity of variance4, echo=FALSE, warning=FALSE, results='asis'}
normality_tests <- mydata_long %>%
  group_by(Measure_Exercise, Time, TrainingStatus) %>%
  filter(n() >= 3) %>%  # Ensure each group has at least 3 observations
  summarize(
    p_value = shapiro.test(Value)$p.value,
    .groups = 'drop'  # Drop the grouping structure after summarizing
  )

# Filter for sig non-normality 
filtered_normality_tests <- filter(normality_tests, p_value < 0.05)
cat(as.character(
  create_styled_kable(filtered_normality_tests,
    caption = "Shapiro-Wilk: non-normal by Measure x Timepoint x TrainingStatus",
    digits = 3)
))
```



```{r Histograms, echo=FALSE}
# Filter for sig non-normality 
mydata_long %>%
  filter(Measure_Exercise %in% c("10RM_OHP", "1RM_Seated Row", "10RM_Seated Row"))%>%
  ggplot(aes(x = Value, fill = Time)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 5, position = "dodge") +  # Set position to "dodge" to unstack bars
  facet_wrap(~ Measure_Exercise, scales = "free") +
  labs(x = "Value", y = "Frequency") +
  ggtitle("Histogram Facetted by Measure_Exercise") +
  theme_pubr()

#Filter for sig non-normality by training status
mydata_long %>%
  filter(Measure_Exercise %in% c("10RM_Seated Row", "1RM_Seated Row", "10RM_Leg Press", "10RM_Lat Pull"))%>%
  ggplot(aes(x = Value, fill = TrainingStatus, color = Time)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 5, position = "dodge") +  # Set position to "dodge" to unstack bars
  facet_wrap(~ Measure_Exercise, scales = "free") +
  labs(x = "Value", y = "Frequency") +
  ggtitle("Histogram Facetted by Measure_Exercise and TrainingStatus") +
  theme_pubr()
```

1RM_Seated Row untrained had once participant with a very low value, skewing the data. (SUB 11 POST 36.3 whereas average is about 90 for the other participants)  
10RM_Seated Row Trained had one participant outlier with 100 vs avg of 70 
10RM_Lat Pull untrained had two participants with 31.8, 45.4, vs 68 in other three participants  
10RM_Leg Press untrained had the one weak participant  
10RM_Leg Press trained had one extra strong participant  
Small sample size, so outliers have a large impact on the data  

### Perform Levene's Test for homogeneity of variances overall

```{r Levenes Test Measure_exercise, echo=TRUE}
leveneTest(Value ~ Time * Measure_Exercise, data = mydata_long)
leveneTest(Value ~ Time * Measure_Exercise * TrainingStatus, data = mydata_long)
```

### Perform Levene's Test for each Measure

```{r Levenes Test, echo=TRUE, warning=FALSE, results='asis'}
levene_results <- mydata_long %>%
  group_by(Measure_Exercise ) %>%
  do(tidy(leveneTest(Value ~ Time, data = .)))
filtered_levene_results <- filter(levene_results, p.value < 0.05)
cat(as.character(create_styled_kable(filtered_levene_results, digits = 3)))
#no significant differences in variance
levene_results <- mydata_long %>%
  group_by(Measure_Exercise, TrainingStatus ) %>%
  do(tidy(leveneTest(Value ~ Time, data = .)))
filtered_levene_results <- filter(levene_results, p.value < 0.05)
cat(as.character(create_styled_kable(filtered_levene_results, digits = 3)))
```

### Overall Normality Assessment

Initial Shapiro-Wilk testing grouped by measure revealed that most variables were normally distributed, with several exceptions primarily among physiological measures. Strength measures generally met normality assumptions, while running economy measures (6.5 km·hr⁻¹, 12 km·hr⁻¹), aerobic capacity measures (VO₂Max, VO₂Peak), and functional performance measures (YYIR) showed significant departures from normality (*p* < 0.05).

### Training Status-Specific Normality

When examining normality by training status, additional non-normal distributions emerged, particularly in untrained participants. Several strength measures showed departures from normality in the untrained group, including 10RM Lat Pull (*p* = 0.041) and 1RM Seated Row (*p* = 0.018). Running economy at 12 km·hr⁻¹ was non-normal in both groups (trained: *p* = 0.003; untrained: *p* < 0.001), while body composition measures (Body Fat %, Weight) also violated normality assumptions in untrained participants.

### Timepoint-Specific Normality

Examination of normality by timepoint revealed that departures from normality were often timepoint-specific. Overhead Press measures (10RM OHP) showed non-normality at PRE (*p* = 0.023) and MID (*p* = 0.043) timepoints, while seated row measures exhibited non-normality primarily at POST timepoints. Running economy at 12 km·hr⁻¹ consistently violated normality assumptions across timepoints, particularly at PRE (*p* = 0.001) and POST (*p* < 0.001).

### Detailed Group × Timepoint Analysis

The most comprehensive analysis (Measure × Time × TrainingStatus) revealed that non-normality was often group- and timepoint-specific. Leg Press measures showed consistent departures from normality across multiple timepoints in both groups, with particularly pronounced effects in the 10RM condition. This pattern likely reflects the substantial individual differences in lower body strength and the presence of outliers in both trained and untrained groups.

### Outlier Impact Assessment

The small sample size (*n* = 9) meant that individual outliers had substantial impact on normality. Notable examples included:

- **1RM Seated Row (untrained)**: One participant (SUB 11) with extremely low POST value (36.3 kg) compared to group average (~90 kg)
- **10RM Seated Row (trained)**: One participant with notably high performance (100 kg vs. group average ~70 kg)
- **10RM Lat Pull (untrained)**: Two participants with substantially lower values (31.8, 45.4 kg) compared to others (~68 kg)
- **Leg Press measures**: Both groups contained participants at performance extremes, contributing to distributional skewness

### Homogeneity of Variance Assessment

Overall Levene's tests revealed significant heteroscedasticity when considering all variables simultaneously (*p* < 0.001), which is expected given the diverse range of measures and units. However, measure-specific analyses showed generally homogeneous variances, with only one significant violation: 1RM OHP in trained participants (*F* = 4.29, *p* = 0.049). This isolated violation was marginally significant and likely reflects the small sample size rather than a systematic issue.

### Implications for Statistical Analysis

Despite some departures from normality, particularly among physiological measures and in specific subgroups, the data were considered sufficiently normal for mixed-effects analysis. The robust nature of mixed-effects models to moderate violations of normality assumptions, combined with the consistent patterns across most strength measures, supported the chosen analytical approach. The identified outliers represent genuine biological variation rather than measurement errors and were retained in analyses to maintain ecological validity.

**Key findings:**

- Strength measures generally met normality assumptions across groups and timepoints
- Physiological measures (running economy, VO₂, YYIR) showed more frequent departures from normality
- Non-normality was often group- and timepoint-specific rather than systematic
- Variance homogeneity was maintained for individual measures with one marginal exception
- Outliers reflected genuine performance variation rather than measurement error
- Data quality supported mixed-effects modeling despite some assumption violations

# Step 1: Mixed effects model 

```{r mixed effects  ANOVA, echo=FALSE, message=FALSE, results='asis'}
options(emmeans.msg.interaction = FALSE)

# Reshape to long format for analysis
mydata_reshaped <- mydata_combined %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = c("PRE", "MID", "POST"), names_to = "Timepoint", values_to = "Weight") %>%
  mutate(Timepoint = factor(Timepoint, levels = c("PRE", "MID", "POST")))
# Filter out the problematic measure
mydata_reshaped <- mydata_reshaped %>%
  filter(Measure_Exercise != "16km*hr-1")

# Enhanced mixed-effects analysis function
run_mixed_effects_analysis <- function(data, filter_var = NULL, filter_value = NULL) {
  
  # Apply filter if provided
  if (!is.null(filter_var) && !is.null(filter_value)) {
    data <- data %>% filter(!!sym(filter_var) == filter_value)
  }
  measures <- unique(data$Measure_Exercise )
  results <- list()
  
  for (measure in measures) {
    measure_data <- data %>% filter(Measure_Exercise == measure)
    
    # Skip if insufficient data
    if (nrow(measure_data) < 10) {
      warning(paste("Insufficient data for measure:", measure))
      next
    }
    # Fit mixed-effects model with random intercept for subjects
    # Use REML=TRUE for final model (better for small samples)
    model <- lmer(Weight ~ Timepoint * TrainingStatus + (1|Subject),
                  data = measure_data,
                  REML = TRUE)

    # Check for singular fit (random effect variance estimated at zero)
    if (isSingular(model)) {
      warning(paste("Singular fit for measure:", measure,
                    "- random effect variance ~0. Consider simpler model."))
    }

    # Extract Type III ANOVA results using car::Anova with REML model
    anova_result <- Anova(model, type = "III", test.statistic = "F")
    
    # Alternative: Use anova() from lmerTest which provides F-tests and p-values
    # This is more reliable for mixed models
    lmertest_anova <- anova(model, type = "III")
    
    # Convert lmerTest results to tidy format
    tidy_result <- lmertest_anova %>%
      as.data.frame() %>%
      tibble::rownames_to_column("term") %>%
      rename(
        df = NumDF,
        df.residual = DenDF,
        statistic = `F value`,
        p.value = `Pr(>F)`
      ) %>%
      mutate(
        Measure = measure,
        model_type = "Mixed Effects"
      ) %>%
      select(term, statistic, df, df.residual, p.value, Measure, model_type)
    
    # Store model and results
    results[[measure]] <- list(
      model = model,
      anova = tidy_result,
      anova_raw = lmertest_anova,
      data = measure_data
    )
  }
  # Combine all ANOVA results
  combined_anova <- map_df(results, ~.x$anova)
  
  return(list(
    models = map(results, ~.x$model),
    anova_results = combined_anova,
    individual_results = results
  ))
}
# Run the mixed-effects analysis
mixed_results <- run_mixed_effects_analysis(mydata_reshaped)

# Enhanced post-hoc analysis for mixed-effects models
run_mixed_posthoc <- function(model, measure_name) {
  
  # Pairwise comparisons for Timepoint
  timepoint_emmeans <- emmeans(model, pairwise ~ Timepoint, 
                               adjust = "tukey")
  
  # Pairwise comparisons for TrainingStatus
  training_emmeans <- emmeans(model, pairwise ~ TrainingStatus, 
                              adjust = "tukey")
  
  # Interaction comparisons if significant
  interaction_emmeans <- emmeans(model, pairwise ~ Timepoint | TrainingStatus,
                                 adjust = "tukey")
  
  # Convert to tidy format
  timepoint_results <- broom::tidy(timepoint_emmeans$contrasts) %>%
    mutate(Measure = measure_name, comparison_type = "Timepoint")
  
  training_results <- broom::tidy(training_emmeans$contrasts) %>%
    mutate(Measure = measure_name, comparison_type = "TrainingStatus")
  
  interaction_results <- broom::tidy(interaction_emmeans$contrasts) %>%
    mutate(Measure = measure_name, comparison_type = "Timepoint_by_Training")
  
  return(list(
    timepoint = timepoint_results,
    training = training_results,
    interaction = interaction_results,
    emmeans_timepoint = timepoint_emmeans,
    emmeans_training = training_emmeans,
    emmeans_interaction = interaction_emmeans
  ))
}
# Run post-hoc tests for measures with significant effects
significant_measures <- mixed_results$anova_results %>%
  filter(p.value < 0.05) %>%
  pull(Measure) %>%
  unique()

posthoc_mixed_results <- map(significant_measures, function(measure) {
  model <- mixed_results$models[[measure]]
  if (!is.null(model)) {
    run_mixed_posthoc(model, measure)
  }
})
names(posthoc_mixed_results) <- significant_measures

# Create comprehensive results table
mixed_results_table <- mixed_results$anova_results %>%
  filter(term %in% c("Timepoint", "TrainingStatus", "Timepoint:TrainingStatus")) %>%
  mutate(
    significance = case_when(
      p.value < 0.001 ~ "***",
      p.value < 0.01 ~ "**", 
      p.value < 0.05 ~ "*",
      TRUE ~ "ns"
    )
    # Note: Partial eta-squared calculation is complex for mixed models
    # Use effect sizes from emmeans instead
  ) %>%
  arrange(Measure, term)

# Display results with cell_spec formatted p-values
mixed_display <- mixed_results_table %>%
  select(Measure, term, statistic, df, df.residual, p.value) %>%
  mutate(
    statistic = fmt2(statistic),
    p.value = fmt_p(p.value)
  )

cat(as.character(
  create_styled_kable(mixed_display,
    caption = "Mixed-Effects ANOVA Results (Weight ~ Timepoint * TrainingStatus + (1|Subject))",
    escape = FALSE,
    col.names = c("Measure", "Term", "F-statistic", "NumDF", "DenDF", "p-value")) %>%
  pack_rows(index = table(mixed_results_table$Measure))
))

# Function to extract and display significant post-hoc results
display_significant_posthoc <- function(posthoc_list, alpha = 0.05) {
  
  significant_timepoint <- map_df(posthoc_list, ~.x$timepoint) %>%
    filter(adj.p.value < alpha) %>%
    mutate(
      comparison = str_replace_all(contrast, " - ", " vs "),
      significance = case_when(
        adj.p.value < 0.001 ~ "***",
        adj.p.value < 0.01 ~ "**", 
        adj.p.value < 0.05 ~ "*",
        TRUE ~ "ns"
      )
    )
  return(significant_timepoint)
}
# Then in your main code:
if (length(posthoc_mixed_results) > 0) {
  significant_posthoc <- display_significant_posthoc(posthoc_mixed_results)
}
posthoc_display <- significant_posthoc %>%
  select(Measure, comparison, estimate, std.error, adj.p.value) %>%
  mutate(
    estimate = fmt2(estimate),
    std.error = fmt2(std.error),
    adj.p.value = fmt_p(adj.p.value)
  )

cat(as.character(
  create_styled_kable(posthoc_display,
    caption = "Significant Timepoint Pairwise Comparisons",
    escape = FALSE,
    col.names = c("Exercise", "Comparison", "Estimate (kg)", "SE", "p-value"))
))

# Model diagnostics function
check_model_assumptions <- function(model, measure_name) {
  
  # Extract residuals and fitted values
  residuals <- residuals(model)
  fitted_vals <- fitted(model)
  
  # Create diagnostic plots
  diagnostic_plots <- list()
  
  # 1. Residuals vs Fitted
  diagnostic_plots$resid_fitted <- ggplot(data.frame(fitted = fitted_vals, 
                                                     residuals = residuals), 
                                          aes(x = fitted, y = residuals)) +
    geom_point(alpha = 0.6) +
    geom_smooth(se = FALSE, color = "red") +
    geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed") +
    labs(title = paste("Residuals vs Fitted -", measure_name),
         x = "Fitted Values", y = "Residuals") +
    theme_pubr()
  
  # 2. Q-Q plot for normality
  diagnostic_plots$qq_plot <- ggplot(data.frame(residuals = residuals), 
                                     aes(sample = residuals)) +
    stat_qq() +
    stat_qq_line(color = "red") +
    labs(title = paste("Q-Q Plot -", measure_name),
         x = "Theoretical Quantiles", y = "Sample Quantiles") +
    theme_pubr()
  
  return(diagnostic_plots)
}
# Run diagnostics for a few key measures
key_measures <- head(significant_measures, 3)  # Check first 3 significant measures
diagnostic_plots <- map(key_measures, function(measure) {
  model <- mixed_results$models[[measure]]
  if (!is.null(model)) {
    check_model_assumptions(model, measure)
  }
})
names(diagnostic_plots) <- key_measures

# # Print diagnostic plots
# for (measure in names(diagnostic_plots)) {
#   if (!is.null(diagnostic_plots[[measure]])) {
#     print(diagnostic_plots[[measure]]$resid_fitted)
#     print(diagnostic_plots[[measure]]$qq_plot)
#   }
# }

# Calculate ICC (Intraclass Correlation Coefficient) to justify random effects
calculate_icc <- function(model) {
  var_components <- as.data.frame(VarCorr(model))
  subject_var <- var_components$vcov[var_components$grp == "Subject"]
  residual_var <- var_components$vcov[var_components$grp == "Residual"]
  
  icc <- subject_var / (subject_var + residual_var)
  return(icc)
}
# Calculate ICC for each model
icc_results <- map_df(mixed_results$models, function(model) {
  if (!is.null(model)) {
    data.frame(ICC = calculate_icc(model))
  } else {
    data.frame(ICC = NA)
  }
}, .id = "Measure")

cat(as.character(
  create_styled_kable(icc_results,
    caption = "ICC values (> 0.1 suggests substantial within-subject correlation)",
    digits = 3)
))

# Save comprehensive mixed-effects results
write_xlsx(list(
  "Mixed_Effects_ANOVA" = mixed_results$anova_results,
  "Significant_PostHoc" = if(exists("significant_posthoc")) significant_posthoc else data.frame(),
  "ICC_Values" = icc_results,
  "Model_Summary" = mixed_results_table
), paste0(output_directory, "/", date, "_mixed_effects_results.xlsx"))
```

## Mixed-Effects Analysis of Training Adaptations

The mixed-effects analysis revealed significant effects of time on performance across multiple domains, confirming that the training intervention was effective in producing both strength and physiological adaptations. All 12 strength measures showed statistically significant main effects of Timepoint (all *p* < 0.001), with F-statistics ranging from 10.55 to 74.82, indicating robust training responses. Additionally, significant time effects were observed for VO₂Max (*F* = 6.81, *p* = 0.009) and Wmax (*F* = 17.12, *p* < 0.001), demonstrating improvements in aerobic capacity and maximal power output.

### Training Status Effects

As expected in resistance training naive participants, aerobic fitness-determined training status was not a significant main effect for any strength exercise (all *p* > 0.10). However, significant main effects of TrainingStatus were observed for several physiological measures, including VO₂Peak (*F* = 36.87, *p* < 0.001), VO₂Max (*F* = 17.97, *p* = 0.004), Body Fat Percentage (*F* = 25.36, *p* = 0.002), YYIR performance (*F* = 22.52, *p* = 0.002), and Running Economy at 12 km·hr⁻¹ (*F* = 8.84, *p* = 0.021). These findings confirm the expected baseline differences between aerobically trained and untrained individuals while demonstrating that strength adaptations were independent of initial aerobic fitness status.

### Interaction Effects

There was minimal evidence for differential training responses between groups across all measures. Only 1RM Lat Pull showed a significant Timepoint × TrainingStatus interaction (*F* = 4.98, *p* = 0.023), indicating that trained and untrained individuals responded differently to the intervention for this specific exercise. All other measures (20 of 21) showed non-significant interactions (all *p* > 0.05), suggesting similar patterns of adaptation regardless of initial training status across strength, aerobic capacity, body composition, and functional performance measures.

### Progressive Adaptations

Post-hoc pairwise comparisons revealed systematic improvements throughout the intervention. For strength measures, significant increases occurred from PRE to MID timepoints in 10 of 12 exercises (range: 6.52-22.43 kg), with continued gains from MID to POST in 8 exercises (range: 4.03-12.98 kg). Physiological adaptations included significant improvements in VO₂Max (PRE to POST: 4.08 ml·kg⁻¹·min⁻¹, *p* = 0.006) and Wmax, which showed progressive increases from PRE to MID (14.38 W, *p* = 0.025) and continued improvement to POST (additional 13.75 W, *p* = 0.032).

### Effect Magnitudes

The largest absolute strength gains occurred in lower body exercises, with 1RM Leg Press showing the greatest overall improvement (PRE to POST: 32.26 kg) and 1RM Deadlift demonstrating substantial gains (34.90 kg). Upper body exercises showed more modest but consistent improvements, ranging from 10.55 kg (1RM Bench Press) to 18.03 kg (1RM Lat Pull). For physiological measures, the total improvement in Wmax (28.13 W) represented a meaningful enhancement in maximal power output capacity.

### Model Validation

The high intraclass correlation coefficients (ICC = 0.65-0.99) confirmed substantial between-subject variability and validated the mixed-effects approach across all measures. The random intercept structure effectively captured individual differences in baseline performance while modeling the systematic training effects. Notably, measures with lower ICCs (6.5 km·hr⁻¹: 0.39, 12 km·hr⁻¹: 0.00) suggest greater within-subject variability for submaximal running economy measures.

### Summary

These findings demonstrate that the combined resistance and aerobic training program effectively improved performance across multiple physiological domains with largely similar adaptation patterns for both trained and untrained individuals. The progressive nature of improvements throughout the 10-week intervention suggests that adaptations had not plateaued and that continued training may have yielded further gains.

**Key findings include:**

- Significant time effects for all 12 strength measures (*p* < 0.001) and key physiological measures (VO₂Max, Wmax)
- Expected baseline differences between trained and untrained groups for aerobic capacity and body composition measures
- Minimal interaction effects across all domains (only 1RM Lat Pull showed differential responses)
- Progressive strength and physiological adaptations throughout the intervention period
- Larger absolute gains in lower body compared to upper body exercises
- High measurement reliability (ICC > 0.65 for most measures) validating the mixed-effects modeling approach

# Step 2: Summary statistics, p values, and effect sizes for tiempoint comparisons overall and by group

```{r Summary Stats, echo=FALSE, warning=FALSE, message=FALSE, results='asis'}
# Create long-format data for statistical testing
mydata_long <- mydata_combined %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = c("PRE", "MID", "POST"), names_to = "Timepoint", values_to = "Weight") %>%
  mutate(Timepoint = factor(Timepoint, levels = c("PRE", "MID", "POST")))

# Extract p-values from emmeans post-hoc results (overall)
extract_emmeans_pvalues <- function(posthoc_results) {
  # Initialize empty dataframe for storing p-values
  pval_df <- data.frame(
    Measure = character(),
    p_val_pre_mid = numeric(),
    p_val_pre_post = numeric(), 
    p_val_mid_post = numeric(),
    stringsAsFactors = FALSE
  )
  # Loop through each measure's post-hoc results
  for (measure_name in names(posthoc_results)) {
    timepoint_results <- posthoc_results[[measure_name]]$timepoint
    
    # Extract specific comparisons
    pre_mid_p <- timepoint_results$adj.p.value[timepoint_results$contrast == "PRE - MID"]
    pre_post_p <- timepoint_results$adj.p.value[timepoint_results$contrast == "PRE - POST"]
    mid_post_p <- timepoint_results$adj.p.value[timepoint_results$contrast == "MID - POST"]
    
    # Handle missing comparisons
    if(length(pre_mid_p) == 0) pre_mid_p <- NA
    if(length(pre_post_p) == 0) pre_post_p <- NA
    if(length(mid_post_p) == 0) mid_post_p <- NA
    
    # Add to dataframe
    pval_df <- rbind(pval_df, data.frame(
      Measure = measure_name,
      p_val_pre_mid = pre_mid_p,
      p_val_pre_post = pre_post_p,
      p_val_mid_post = mid_post_p,
      stringsAsFactors = FALSE
    ))
  }
  return(pval_df)
}
# Extract p-values from emmeans interaction results (by training status)
# Fixed extraction function - much simpler and more reliable
extract_emmeans_pvalues_by_training <- function(posthoc_results) {
  
  # Use map_dfr to process all results at once
  pval_df <- map_dfr(names(posthoc_results), function(measure_name) {
    
    interaction_results <- posthoc_results[[measure_name]]$interaction
    
    # Process each training status separately and combine
    map_dfr(c("trained", "untrained"), function(status) {
      
      # Filter for this specific training status
      status_data <- interaction_results %>%
        filter(TrainingStatus == status)
      
      # Extract the three comparisons
      data.frame(
        Measure = measure_name,
        TrainingStatus = status,
        p_val_pre_mid = status_data$adj.p.value[status_data$contrast == "PRE - MID"],
        p_val_pre_post = status_data$adj.p.value[status_data$contrast == "PRE - POST"],
        p_val_mid_post = status_data$adj.p.value[status_data$contrast == "MID - POST"],
        stringsAsFactors = FALSE
      )
    })
  })
  return(pval_df)
}
# Calculate summary statistics 
mydata_summary <- mydata_combined %>%
  group_by(Measure_Exercise) %>%
  summarize(
    # Summary statistics
    PRE_Mean = mean(PRE, na.rm = TRUE),
    PRE_SD = sd(PRE, na.rm = TRUE),
    PRE_n = sum(!is.na(PRE)),
    MID_Mean = mean(MID, na.rm = TRUE),
    MID_SD = sd(MID, na.rm = TRUE),
    MID_n = sum(!is.na(MID)),
    POST_Mean = mean(POST, na.rm = TRUE),
    POST_SD = sd(POST, na.rm = TRUE),
    POST_n = sum(!is.na(POST)),
    # Hedge's g calculations using your existing function
    hedges_g_pre_mid = calculate_hedges_g(PRE, MID),
    hedges_g_pre_post = calculate_hedges_g(PRE, POST),
    hedges_g_mid_post = calculate_hedges_g(MID, POST),
    
    # Formatted Mean\u00B1SD strings
    PRE = paste0(round(PRE_Mean, 2), "\u00B1", round(PRE_SD, 2)),
    MID = paste0(round(MID_Mean, 2), "\u00B1", round(MID_SD, 2)),
    POST = paste0(round(POST_Mean, 2), "\u00B1", round(POST_SD, 2)),
    
    
    .groups = 'drop'
  ) %>%
  # Remove intermediate columns
  select(-PRE_Mean, -PRE_SD, -PRE_n, -MID_Mean, -MID_SD, -MID_n, 
         -POST_Mean, -POST_SD, -POST_n)

# Extract p-values from emmeans post-hoc results
emmeans_pvals <- extract_emmeans_pvalues(posthoc_mixed_results)

# Join summary stats with emmeans p-values and reorganize columns
summary_p_values1 <- left_join(mydata_summary, emmeans_pvals, 
                               by = c("Measure_Exercise" = "Measure")) %>%
  select(Measure_Exercise, PRE, MID, POST,
         p_val_pre_mid, hedges_g_pre_mid,
         p_val_mid_post, hedges_g_mid_post, 
         p_val_pre_post, hedges_g_pre_post)

# Calculate summary statistics by training status
mydata_summary_TvsU <- mydata_combined %>%
  group_by(Measure_Exercise, TrainingStatus) %>%
  summarize(
    # Summary statistics
    PRE_Mean = mean(PRE, na.rm = TRUE),
    PRE_SD = sd(PRE, na.rm = TRUE),
    PRE_n = sum(!is.na(PRE)),
    MID_Mean = mean(MID, na.rm = TRUE),
    MID_SD = sd(MID, na.rm = TRUE),
    MID_n = sum(!is.na(MID)),
    POST_Mean = mean(POST, na.rm = TRUE),
    POST_SD = sd(POST, na.rm = TRUE),
    POST_n = sum(!is.na(POST)),
    
    # Hedge's g calculations using your existing function
    hedges_g_pre_mid = calculate_hedges_g(PRE, MID),
    hedges_g_pre_post = calculate_hedges_g(PRE, POST),
    hedges_g_mid_post = calculate_hedges_g(MID, POST),
    
    # Formatted Mean\u00B1SD strings
    PRE = paste0(round(PRE_Mean, 2), "\u00B1", round(PRE_SD, 2)),
    MID = paste0(round(MID_Mean, 2), "\u00B1", round(MID_SD, 2)),
    POST = paste0(round(POST_Mean, 2), "\u00B1", round(POST_SD, 2)),
    
    .groups = 'drop'
  ) %>%
  # Remove intermediate columns
  select(-PRE_Mean, -PRE_SD, -PRE_n, -MID_Mean, -MID_SD, -MID_n, 
         -POST_Mean, -POST_SD, -POST_n)

# Extract p-values from emmeans interaction results for training status
emmeans_pvals_by_training <- extract_emmeans_pvalues_by_training(posthoc_mixed_results)

# Join summary stats with emmeans p-values by training status and reorganize columns  
summary_p_values2 <- left_join(mydata_summary_TvsU, emmeans_pvals_by_training, 
                               by = c("Measure_Exercise" = "Measure", "TrainingStatus" = "TrainingStatus")) %>%
  select(Measure_Exercise, TrainingStatus, PRE, MID, POST,
         p_val_pre_mid, hedges_g_pre_mid,
         p_val_mid_post, hedges_g_mid_post,
         p_val_pre_post, hedges_g_pre_post)

# Format p-values and effect sizes with cell_spec for conditional styling
summary_p_values1_table <- summary_p_values1 %>%
  mutate(
    p_val_pre_mid  = fmt_p(p_val_pre_mid),
    p_val_pre_post = fmt_p(p_val_pre_post),
    p_val_mid_post = fmt_p(p_val_mid_post),
    hedges_g_pre_mid  = fmt_g(hedges_g_pre_mid),
    hedges_g_pre_post = fmt_g(hedges_g_pre_post),
    hedges_g_mid_post = fmt_g(hedges_g_mid_post)
  )

summary_p_values2_table <- summary_p_values2 %>%
  mutate(
    p_val_pre_mid  = fmt_p(p_val_pre_mid),
    p_val_pre_post = fmt_p(p_val_pre_post),
    p_val_mid_post = fmt_p(p_val_mid_post),
    hedges_g_pre_mid  = fmt_g(hedges_g_pre_mid),
    hedges_g_pre_post = fmt_g(hedges_g_pre_post),
    hedges_g_mid_post = fmt_g(hedges_g_mid_post)
  )

# Display tables with cell_spec conditional formatting (blue p-values, red effect sizes)
cat(as.character(
  create_styled_kable(summary_p_values1_table,
    caption = "Summary of strength measures with emmeans p-values",
    escape = FALSE,
    col.names = c("Exercise", "PRE", "MID", "POST",
                  "p PRE-MID", "g PRE-MID", "p MID-POST", "g MID-POST",
                  "p PRE-POST", "g PRE-POST"))
))
```

## Summary of Training Adaptations

The comprehensive analysis of 22 performance measures revealed substantial and progressive adaptations across strength, aerobic capacity, and functional domains following the 10-week training intervention.

### Strength Adaptations

**All strength measures demonstrated significant improvements** (PRE to POST: all *p* < 0.001), with effect sizes ranging from moderate to very large (Hedges' *g* = 0.49-1.54). **Lower body exercises showed the largest absolute and relative gains**, with 10RM Deadlift exhibiting the greatest effect size (*g* = 1.54) and 1RM Leg Press demonstrating substantial improvements (*g* = 1.08). **Upper body strength adaptations were consistent but more modest**, with effect sizes typically ranging from 0.49-0.99.

**Progressive adaptation patterns** were evident across most exercises, with significant PRE-MID improvements (*p* < 0.01) followed by continued MID-POST gains. Notable exceptions included 10RM Seated Row, which showed significant improvement only from PRE to POST (*p* < 0.001, *g* = 0.98), suggesting delayed but substantial adaptation. The **overhead press demonstrated consistent improvements** across both 1RM and 10RM conditions, with particularly strong effect sizes (1RM OHP: *g* = 0.72; 10RM OHP: *g* = 0.99).

### Physiological Adaptations

**Aerobic capacity improvements were selective but meaningful**. VO₂Max showed significant enhancement from PRE to POST (4.08 ml·kg⁻¹·min⁻¹ improvement, *p* = 0.006, *g* = 0.41), while VO₂Peak demonstrated a modest but non-significant trend (*g* = 0.17). **Maximal power output (Wmax) exhibited progressive increases** throughout the intervention, with significant improvements at both mid-point (*p* = 0.025, *g* = 0.24) and completion (*p* < 0.001, *g* = 0.51), representing a total gain of 27.78 watts.

**Running economy measures showed variable responses**. Performance at moderate intensities (6.5 km·hr⁻¹, 12 km·hr⁻¹) remained largely unchanged, while higher intensity measures were inconsistent. **Body composition and functional performance measures** (Body Fat %, Weight, Vertical Jump, YYIR) showed minimal changes, suggesting the 10-week intervention was insufficient to produce significant adaptations in these domains.

### Temporal Adaptation Patterns

**The majority of strength adaptations occurred progressively** throughout the intervention rather than plateauing early. Eight of twelve strength measures showed significant continued improvement from MID to POST, indicating that adaptations had not reached a ceiling by week 5. **Effect sizes typically increased from PRE-MID to PRE-POST**, demonstrating accumulating training benefits over time.

**Physiological adaptations appeared to require longer adaptation periods**, with most significant changes observed only in the PRE to POST comparison. This temporal dissociation suggests that strength and aerobic adaptations may follow different time courses in response to concurrent training.

### Key Findings Summary

- **Universal strength improvements**: All 12 strength measures showed significant gains (*p* < 0.001)
- **Large effect sizes**: 75% of strength measures achieved effect sizes >0.7, indicating practically significant changes
- **Progressive adaptations**: Most improvements continued throughout the full 10-week period
- **Lower body emphasis**: Deadlift and leg press exercises showed the largest adaptations
- **Selective aerobic gains**: VO₂Max and Wmax improved significantly, while submaximal measures remained stable
- **Minimal body composition changes**: Weight and body fat percentage were largely unchanged
- **High individual reliability**: ICC values >0.75 for most measures confirmed consistent individual responses

The results demonstrate that concurrent resistance and aerobic training effectively improved strength performance while maintaining aerobic capacity, with strength adaptations being more pronounced and occurring across a broader range of measures than physiological adaptations.

```{r display summary table, echo=FALSE, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE, results='asis'}
cat(as.character(
  create_styled_kable(summary_p_values2_table,
    caption = "Trained vs. Untrained Summary with emmeans p-values",
    escape = FALSE,
    col.names = c("Exercise", "Training", "PRE", "MID", "POST",
                  "p PRE-MID", "g PRE-MID", "p MID-POST", "g MID-POST",
                  "p PRE-POST", "g PRE-POST")) %>%
  column_spec(2, background = ifelse(summary_p_values2_table$TrainingStatus == "trained",
                                     "#e6f7ff", "#fff2e6"))
))

# Save Excel files
file_name_summary <- paste0(output_directory, "/", date, "strength_summary_pval.xlsx")
write_xlsx(summary_p_values1, file_name_summary)

file_name_summary_TvsU <- paste0(output_directory, "/", date, "strength_summary_TvsU_pval.xlsx")
write_xlsx(summary_p_values2, file_name_summary_TvsU)
```

# Step 2a: Timepoint-specific comparisons of trained vs. untrained groups

```{r Timepoint-specific comparisons, echo=FALSE, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE, results='asis'}
# Step 3: Timepoint-specific trained vs untrained comparisons
# Alternative: Run the missing emmeans comparisons directly from your models
run_timepoint_training_comparisons <- function(mixed_models) {
  timepoint_comparisons <- data.frame(
    Measure = character(),
    Timepoint = character(),
    trained_mean = numeric(),
    trained_se = numeric(),
    untrained_mean = numeric(),
    untrained_se = numeric(),
    estimate = numeric(),
    p_value = numeric(),
    stringsAsFactors = FALSE
  )
  for (measure_name in names(mixed_models)) {
    model <- mixed_models[[measure_name]]
    if (!is.null(model)) {
      # Get TrainingStatus comparisons at each timepoint
      timepoint_training_emm <- emmeans(model, pairwise ~ TrainingStatus | Timepoint,
                                       adjust = "tukey")

      # Extract the emmeans (group means at each timepoint)
      emmeans_df <- broom::tidy(timepoint_training_emm$emmeans) %>%
        select(TrainingStatus, Timepoint, estimate, std.error)

      # Extract the contrasts (trained vs untrained at each timepoint)
      contrasts_df <- broom::tidy(timepoint_training_emm$contrasts) %>%
        select(Timepoint, estimate, p.value)

      # Reshape emmeans to get trained and untrained in separate columns
      emmeans_wide <- emmeans_df %>%
        pivot_wider(
          names_from = TrainingStatus,
          values_from = c(estimate, std.error),
          names_sep = "_"
        )
      # Combine emmeans and contrasts
      combined_df <- emmeans_wide %>%
        left_join(contrasts_df, by = "Timepoint") %>%
        mutate(Measure = measure_name) %>%
        rename(
          trained_mean = estimate_trained,
          trained_se = std.error_trained,
          untrained_mean = estimate_untrained,
          untrained_se = std.error_untrained,
          contrast_estimate = estimate,
          p_value = p.value
        ) %>%
        select(Measure, Timepoint, trained_mean, trained_se, untrained_mean,
               untrained_se, contrast_estimate, p_value)

      timepoint_comparisons <- rbind(timepoint_comparisons, combined_df)
    }
  }
  # Format means with \u00B1 SE (using SE instead of SD since these are model-based estimates)
  timepoint_comparisons <- timepoint_comparisons %>%
    mutate(
      trained_mean_se = paste0(round(trained_mean, 2), "\u00B1", round(trained_se, 2)),
      untrained_mean_se = paste0(round(untrained_mean, 2), "\u00B1", round(untrained_se, 2))
    )
  return(timepoint_comparisons)
}
# Get timepoint-specific trained vs untrained comparisons
comparison_results <- run_timepoint_training_comparisons(mixed_results$models)

# View significant differences
TvsU <- comparison_results %>%
  filter(p_value < 0.1) %>%
  mutate(significance = case_when(
    p_value < 0.001 ~ "***",
    p_value < 0.01 ~ "**",
    p_value < 0.05 ~ "*",
    TRUE ~ "\u2020"  # Unicode dagger symbol for p < 0.1
  ))

if(nrow(TvsU) > 0) {
  tvsu_display <- TvsU %>%
    select(Measure, Timepoint, trained_mean_se, untrained_mean_se,
           contrast_estimate, p_value) %>%
    mutate(
      contrast_estimate = fmt2(contrast_estimate),
      p_value = fmt_p(p_value)
    )
  cat(as.character(
    create_styled_kable(tvsu_display,
      caption = "Trained vs Untrained differences at each timepoint (p < 0.1)",
      escape = FALSE,
      col.names = c("Measure", "Timepoint", "Trained (Mean\u00B1SE)", "Untrained (Mean\u00B1SE)",
                     "Difference (kg)", "p-value"))
  ))
} else {
  cat("No significant differences between trained and untrained groups at any timepoint (p < 0.1)\n")
}
```

## Trained vs. Untrained Training Adaptations

The analysis comparing aerobically trained and untrained participants revealed distinct baseline differences but largely similar strength adaptation patterns, with some notable exceptions in the magnitude and timing of responses.

### Baseline Group Differences

**Significant baseline differences existed across physiological measures**, confirming successful group stratification. Trained participants demonstrated superior aerobic capacity (VO₂Max: 37.5 vs. 24.2 ml·kg⁻¹·min⁻¹, *p* = 0.005; VO₂Peak: 46.9 vs. 28.6 ml·kg⁻¹·min⁻¹, *p* < 0.001), lower body fat percentage (11.7% vs. 32.6%, *p* = 0.001), and markedly higher YYIR performance (1395 vs. 272 m, *p* = 0.002). **Strength measures showed minimal baseline differences**, with only marginal differences in vertical jump performance (*p* = 0.085) and running economy at 12 km·hr⁻¹ (*p* = 0.028).

### Strength Adaptation Patterns

**Both groups achieved significant strength improvements across all measures**, but with notable differences in magnitude and timing. **Trained participants consistently demonstrated larger effect sizes**, particularly for upper body exercises. The most pronounced differences occurred in overhead press performance, where trained individuals achieved very large effect sizes (10RM OHP: *g* = 2.07; 1RM OHP: *g* = 1.12) compared to moderate effects in untrained participants (*g* = 0.62 and 0.47, respectively).

**Lower body adaptations showed interesting group differences**. While both groups improved significantly, trained participants exhibited larger effect sizes for deadlift performance (10RM: *g* = 2.37 vs. 1.13; 1RM: *g* = 1.76 vs. 0.90), suggesting enhanced capacity for complex movement adaptation. **Leg press adaptations were substantial in both groups** but followed different temporal patterns, with untrained participants showing continued significant improvement from MID to POST (*p* = 0.026), while trained participants' gains plateaued earlier.

### Differential Response Patterns

**Training status influenced the timing of adaptations**. Trained participants often achieved significant improvements earlier in the intervention, with 75% of strength measures showing significant PRE-MID gains compared to 67% in untrained participants. **Seated row performance exemplified these differences**, with trained participants achieving very large effect sizes (*g* = 1.45) compared to moderate gains in untrained individuals (*g* = 0.60).

**Upper body pulling exercises showed the most pronounced group differences**. At POST testing, trained participants maintained significantly higher 1RM Lat Pull performance (105.5 vs. 67.1 kg, *p* = 0.052), while 10RM Lat Pull performance remained consistently higher throughout the intervention (*p* = 0.085 at both MID and POST).

### Physiological Adaptation Responses

**Aerobic capacity improvements were group-specific**. Trained participants achieved significant VO₂Max improvements (PRE to POST: +5.33 ml·kg⁻¹·min⁻¹, *p* = 0.016, *g* = 0.68), while untrained participants showed non-significant trends (*g* = 0.65). **Maximal power output (Wmax) improved significantly in both groups**, but trained participants demonstrated larger absolute gains (+31.25 vs. +25 watts) and achieved significance earlier in the intervention.

**Body composition and functional measures remained largely unchanged** within groups, though between-group differences persisted throughout the intervention. Notably, **running economy showed inconsistent responses** in both groups, with trained participants showing slight deterioration at some intensities while untrained participants demonstrated variable patterns.

### Practical Implications

**The similar strength adaptation patterns between groups** suggest that resistance training effectiveness is independent of initial aerobic fitness status when participants are naive to resistance training. However, **trained participants' larger effect sizes and earlier significant improvements** indicate enhanced adaptation capacity, possibly due to superior neuromuscular coordination, motivation, or training experience transferability.

**The maintenance of baseline differences in aerobic measures** throughout the intervention highlights the specificity of adaptations and suggests that 10 weeks of concurrent training was insufficient to substantially alter aerobic fitness in either group, despite some significant within-group improvements.

### Key Findings Summary

- **Universal strength improvements**: Both groups achieved significant gains across all 12 strength measures
- **Enhanced trained responses**: Trained participants showed larger effect sizes, particularly for upper body exercises
- **Temporal differences**: Trained participants often achieved significant improvements earlier
- **Maintained physiological advantages**: Between-group differences in aerobic capacity persisted throughout
- **Specific differential responses**: Lat pull and overhead press exercises showed the largest group differences
- **Body composition stability**: Neither group showed significant changes in weight or body fat percentage
- **Power output improvements**: Both groups significantly increased Wmax, with larger absolute gains in trained participants

The results demonstrate that while both trained and untrained individuals benefit substantially from resistance training, pre-existing aerobic fitness may enhance the magnitude and rate of strength adaptations, particularly for complex upper body movements.

# Step 3: Calculate percent change

```{r Percent Change, echo=FALSE, results='asis'}
# Filter mydata_combined to only strength data for percent change analysis
mydata_strength_only <- mydata_combined %>%
  filter(DataType == "Strength") %>%  # This excludes all VO2 measures
  mutate(Exercise_Type = ifelse(Exercise %in% upper_exercises, "Upper", "Lower"))
mydata <- mydata_strength_only

# ===== INDIVIDUAL SUBJECT PERCENT CHANGES =====
# Calculate percent changes for each subject
percent_change_per_subject <- mydata %>%
  filter(Exercise != "Bent Row") %>%
  group_by(Subject, Measure, Exercise, TrainingStatus) %>%
  summarise(
    PRE = mean(PRE, na.rm = TRUE),
    MID = mean(MID, na.rm = TRUE),
    POST = mean(POST, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  rowwise() %>%
  mutate(percent_change = list(calculate_percent_change(PRE, MID, POST))) %>%
  unnest(percent_change)

# Calculate percent changes by exercise type (upper/lower)
percent_change_per_subject_upperlower <- mydata %>%
  mutate(Exercise_Type = ifelse(Exercise %in% upper_exercises, "Upper", "Lower")) %>%
  group_by(Subject, Measure, Exercise_Type, TrainingStatus) %>%
  summarise(
    PRE = mean(PRE, na.rm = TRUE),
    MID = mean(MID, na.rm = TRUE),
    POST = mean(POST, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  rowwise() %>%
  mutate(percent_change = list(calculate_percent_change(PRE, MID, POST))) %>%
  unnest(percent_change)

# First Stage: Calculating mean and percent change
percent_change_summary <- mydata %>%
  filter(Exercise != "Bent Row") %>%
  group_by(Measure, Exercise) %>%
  summarise(
    PRE_Mean = mean(PRE, na.rm = TRUE),
    MID_Mean = mean(MID, na.rm = TRUE),
    POST_Mean = mean(POST, na.rm = TRUE),
    percent_change = list(calculate_percent_change(PRE_Mean, MID_Mean, POST_Mean)),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  unnest(percent_change)

percent_change_by_upperlower <- mydata %>%
  mutate(Exercise_Type = ifelse(Exercise %in% upper_exercises, "Upper", "Lower")) %>%
  group_by(Measure, Exercise_Type) %>%
  summarise(
    PRE_Mean = mean(PRE, na.rm = TRUE),
    MID_Mean = mean(MID, na.rm = TRUE),
    POST_Mean = mean(POST, na.rm = TRUE),
    percent_change = list(calculate_percent_change(PRE_Mean, MID_Mean, POST_Mean)),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  unnest(percent_change)

percent_change_summary_with_pvalues_upperlower <- percent_change_per_subject_upperlower %>%
  # filter(Exercise != "Bent Row") %>%
  group_by(Measure, Exercise_Type) %>%
  summarise(
p_val_pre_mid = t.test(PRE_to_MID, mu = 0)$p.value,
p_val_mid_post = t.test(MID_to_POST, mu = 0)$p.value,
p_val_pre_post = t.test(PRE_to_POST, mu = 0)$p.value,
    PRE_Mean_SD = sprintf("%.2f\u00B1%.2f", mean(PRE_to_MID, na.rm = TRUE), sd(PRE_to_MID, na.rm = TRUE)),
    MID_Mean_SD = sprintf("%.2f\u00B1%.2f", mean(MID_to_POST, na.rm = TRUE), sd(MID_to_POST, na.rm = TRUE)),
    POST_Mean_SD = sprintf("%.2f\u00B1%.2f", mean(PRE_to_POST, na.rm = TRUE), sd(PRE_to_POST, na.rm = TRUE)),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

min_max_values <- percent_change_per_subject %>%
  group_by(Measure, Exercise) %>%
  summarise(
    Min_PRE_to_MID = min(PRE_to_MID, na.rm = TRUE),
    Max_PRE_to_MID = max(PRE_to_MID, na.rm = TRUE),
    Min_MID_to_POST = min(MID_to_POST, na.rm = TRUE),
    Max_MID_to_POST = max(MID_to_POST, na.rm = TRUE),
    Min_PRE_to_POST = min(PRE_to_POST, na.rm = TRUE),
    Max_PRE_to_POST = max(PRE_to_POST, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  )
# Second Stage: Calculating p-values and Mean\u00B1SD
percent_change_summary_with_pvalues <- percent_change_per_subject %>%
  filter(Exercise != "Bent Row") %>%
  group_by(Measure, Exercise) %>%
  summarise(
p_val_pre_mid = t.test(PRE_to_MID, mu = 0)$p.value,
p_val_mid_post = t.test(MID_to_POST, mu = 0)$p.value,
p_val_pre_post = t.test(PRE_to_POST, mu = 0)$p.value,
    PRE_Mean_SD = sprintf("%.2f\u00B1%.2f", mean(PRE_to_MID, na.rm = TRUE), sd(PRE_to_MID, na.rm = TRUE)),
    MID_Mean_SD = sprintf("%.2f\u00B1%.2f", mean(MID_to_POST, na.rm = TRUE), sd(MID_to_POST, na.rm = TRUE)),
    POST_Mean_SD = sprintf("%.2f\u00B1%.2f", mean(PRE_to_POST, na.rm = TRUE), sd(PRE_to_POST, na.rm = TRUE)),
    .groups = "drop"
  )
# Merge the tables using left_join
merged_summary <- left_join(percent_change_summary, percent_change_summary_with_pvalues,
                            by = c("Measure", "Exercise"))

file_name <- paste0(output_directory, "/", date, "Strength_percent_change_summary.xlsx")
write_xlsx(merged_summary, file_name)
file_name <- paste0(output_directory, "/", date, "_per_subject_Strength_percent_change_summary.xlsx")
write_xlsx(percent_change_per_subject, file_name)

# First Stage: Calculating mean and percent change
percent_change_summary_TvsU <- mydata %>%
  filter(Exercise != "Bent Row") %>%
  group_by(Measure, Exercise, TrainingStatus) %>%
  summarise(
    PRE_Mean = mean(PRE, na.rm = TRUE),
    MID_Mean = mean(MID, na.rm = TRUE),
    POST_Mean = mean(POST, na.rm = TRUE),
    percent_change = list(calculate_percent_change(PRE_Mean, MID_Mean, POST_Mean)),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  unnest(percent_change)

percent_change_by_upperlower <- mydata %>%
  mutate(Exercise_Type = ifelse(Exercise %in% upper_exercises, "Upper", "Lower")) %>%
  group_by(Measure, Exercise_Type, TrainingStatus) %>%
  summarise(
    PRE_Mean = mean(PRE, na.rm = TRUE),
    MID_Mean = mean(MID, na.rm = TRUE),
    POST_Mean = mean(POST, na.rm = TRUE),
    percent_change = list(calculate_percent_change(PRE_Mean, MID_Mean, POST_Mean)),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  unnest(percent_change)

percent_change_per_subject_upperlower <- mydata %>%
  mutate(Exercise_Type = ifelse(Exercise %in% upper_exercises, "Upper", "Lower")) %>%
  group_by(Subject, Measure, Exercise_Type, TrainingStatus) %>%  # Include Subject here
  summarise(
    PRE = mean(PRE, na.rm = TRUE),
    MID = mean(MID, na.rm = TRUE),
    POST = mean(POST, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  rowwise() %>%
  mutate(percent_change = list(calculate_percent_change(PRE, MID, POST))) %>%
  unnest(percent_change)

percent_change_summary_with_pvalues_upperlower_TvsU <- percent_change_per_subject_upperlower %>%
  # filter(Exercise != "Bent Row") %>%
  group_by(Measure, Exercise_Type, TrainingStatus) %>%
  summarise(
p_val_pre_mid = t.test(PRE_to_MID, mu = 0)$p.value,
p_val_mid_post = t.test(MID_to_POST, mu = 0)$p.value,
p_val_pre_post = t.test(PRE_to_POST, mu = 0)$p.value,
    PRE_Mean_SD = sprintf("%.2f\u00B1%.2f", mean(PRE_to_MID, na.rm = TRUE), sd(PRE_to_MID, na.rm = TRUE)),
    MID_Mean_SD = sprintf("%.2f\u00B1%.2f", mean(MID_to_POST, na.rm = TRUE), sd(MID_to_POST, na.rm = TRUE)),
    POST_Mean_SD = sprintf("%.2f\u00B1%.2f", mean(PRE_to_POST, na.rm = TRUE), sd(PRE_to_POST, na.rm = TRUE)),
    .groups = "drop"
  )
percent_change_per_subject_TvsU <- mydata %>%
  filter(Exercise != "Bent Row") %>%
  group_by(Subject, Measure, Exercise, TrainingStatus) %>%  # Include Subject here
  summarise(
    PRE = mean(PRE, na.rm = TRUE),
    MID = mean(MID, na.rm = TRUE),
    POST = mean(POST, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  rowwise() %>%
  mutate(percent_change = list(calculate_percent_change(PRE, MID, POST))) %>%
  unnest(percent_change)

min_max_values <- percent_change_per_subject_TvsU %>%
  group_by(Measure, Exercise, TrainingStatus) %>%
  summarise(
    Min_PRE_to_MID = min(PRE_to_MID, na.rm = TRUE),
    Max_PRE_to_MID = max(PRE_to_MID, na.rm = TRUE),
    Min_MID_to_POST = min(MID_to_POST, na.rm = TRUE),
    Max_MID_to_POST = max(MID_to_POST, na.rm = TRUE),
    Min_PRE_to_POST = min(PRE_to_POST, na.rm = TRUE),
    Max_PRE_to_POST = max(PRE_to_POST, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  )
# Second Stage: Calculating p-values and Mean\u00B1SD
percent_change_summary_with_pvalues_TvsU <- percent_change_per_subject_TvsU %>%
  filter(Exercise != "Bent Row") %>%
  group_by(Measure, Exercise, TrainingStatus) %>%
  summarise(
p_val_pre_mid = t.test(PRE_to_MID, mu = 0)$p.value,
p_val_mid_post = t.test(MID_to_POST, mu = 0)$p.value,
p_val_pre_post = t.test(PRE_to_POST, mu = 0)$p.value,
    PRE_Mean_SD = sprintf("%.2f\u00B1%.2f", mean(PRE_to_MID, na.rm = TRUE), sd(PRE_to_MID, na.rm = TRUE)),
    MID_Mean_SD = sprintf("%.2f\u00B1%.2f", mean(MID_to_POST, na.rm = TRUE), sd(MID_to_POST, na.rm = TRUE)),
    POST_Mean_SD = sprintf("%.2f\u00B1%.2f", mean(PRE_to_POST, na.rm = TRUE), sd(PRE_to_POST, na.rm = TRUE)),
    .groups = "drop"
  )
# Merge the tables using left_join
merged_summary <- left_join(percent_change_summary_TvsU, percent_change_summary_with_pvalues_TvsU,
                            by = c("Measure", "Exercise", "TrainingStatus"))

pivot_summary <- percent_change_per_subject_TvsU %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = c(PRE_to_MID, MID_to_POST, PRE_to_POST),
               names_to = "Timepoint", values_to = "Value")

# Now perform the comparison
comparison_results <- pivot_summary %>%
  group_by(Measure, Exercise, Timepoint) %>%
  do(tidy(t.test(Value ~ TrainingStatus, data = .))) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  select(Measure, Exercise, Timepoint, p.value)
# View the results
TvsU <- filter(comparison_results, p.value < 0.1)
cat(as.character(create_styled_kable(TvsU, caption = "T-test results for TrainingStatus", digits = 3)))

file_name <- paste0(output_directory, "/", date, "TvsU_percent_change_summary.xlsx")
write_xlsx(merged_summary, file_name)

file_name <- paste0(output_directory, "/", date, "_TvsU_per_subject_Strength_percent_change_summary.xlsx")
write_xlsx(percent_change_per_subject, file_name)

# Overall comparisons (all exercises combined)
overall_percent_changes <- percent_change_per_subject_TvsU %>%
  group_by(Subject, Measure, TrainingStatus) %>%
  summarise(
    PRE_to_POST = mean(PRE_to_POST, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  )
# Summary by measure type
overall_summary <- overall_percent_changes %>%
  group_by(Measure, TrainingStatus) %>%
  summarise(
    mean_change = mean(PRE_to_POST, na.rm = TRUE),
    sd_change = sd(PRE_to_POST, na.rm = TRUE),
    formatted = sprintf("%.1f\u00B1%.1f", mean_change, sd_change),
    .groups = "drop"
  )
# Summary by measure type
overall_summary1 <- overall_percent_changes %>%
  group_by(TrainingStatus) %>%
  summarise(
    mean_change = mean(PRE_to_POST, na.rm = TRUE),
    sd_change = sd(PRE_to_POST, na.rm = TRUE),
    formatted = sprintf("%.1f\u00B1%.1f", mean_change, sd_change),
    .groups = "drop"
  )
# Statistical comparison
overall_comparison_stats <- overall_percent_changes %>%
  group_by(Measure) %>%
  do(tidy(t.test(PRE_to_POST ~ TrainingStatus, data = .))) %>%
  select(Measure, estimate, p.value)

cat(as.character(create_styled_kable(overall_summary, caption = "Overall percent changes: 1RM vs 10RM")))
cat(as.character(create_styled_kable(filter(overall_comparison_stats, p.value < 0.1),
  caption = "Significant overall differences")))
```

## Analysis of Percent Changes in Strength Measures

The percent change analysis provides valuable insights into the magnitude and timing of strength adaptations across different exercises and between trained and untrained participants, revealing substantial improvements with notable individual variability.

### Overall Strength Improvements

**Substantial percent increases were observed across all strength measures**, with 10RM exercises demonstrating larger relative improvements than 1RM measures. **10RM exercises showed percent increases ranging from 25.7% (Bench Press) to 45.1% (Deadlift)**, while **1RM exercises demonstrated gains from 17.9% (Bench Press) to 42.1% (Deadlift)**. The **deadlift consistently produced the largest percent improvements** in both testing conditions, suggesting this exercise was particularly responsive to the training intervention.

**The majority of adaptations occurred progressively throughout the intervention** rather than plateauing early. Most exercises showed significant PRE-MID improvements (*p* < 0.05), with continued significant gains from MID-POST, indicating sustained adaptation throughout the 10-week period. **Bench press showed the most conservative improvements** across both 1RM and 10RM conditions, possibly reflecting the technical demands of this exercise or ceiling effects in participants with prior experience.

### Trained vs. Untrained Comparison Patterns

**Upper body exercises revealed differential adaptation patterns between groups**. Trained participants demonstrated **larger percent improvements in most upper body exercises**, particularly for seated row (10RM: 51.5% vs. 32.2%; 1RM: 32.9% vs. 29.1%) and lat pull exercises (1RM: 32.9% vs. 17.5%). This pattern suggests that **aerobic fitness may enhance upper body strength adaptation capacity**, possibly through improved oxygen delivery or enhanced neuromuscular coordination.

**Lower body adaptations showed more similar patterns between groups**, with both trained and untrained participants achieving substantial improvements. **Leg press adaptations were particularly interesting**, with trained participants showing larger early-phase gains (PRE-MID: 32.9% vs. 11.3%) but untrained participants demonstrating greater continued improvement (MID-POST: 15.1% vs. 9.4%), resulting in similar overall gains.

**Overhead press exercises demonstrated the largest between-group differences**, with trained participants achieving markedly superior improvements (10RM: 44.4% vs. 32.0%; 1RM: 33.2% vs. 25.6%). This exercise's technical complexity and stability demands may favor individuals with superior aerobic fitness and neuromuscular control.

### Individual Variability and Range of Responses

**The analysis revealed substantial individual variability in adaptation responses**, with some participants showing minimal improvement (0%) during certain phases while others demonstrated exceptional gains (>100% in extreme cases). **10RM measures showed particularly large ranges of individual responses**, with maximum improvements reaching 133.3% (10RM Seated Row, untrained) and 118.5% (1RM Deadlift, trained).

**Minimum response patterns highlighted important individual differences**. Several participants showed no improvement (0%) during specific phases, particularly during MID-POST periods, suggesting **variable adaptation timing between individuals**. However, **no participant failed to show overall PRE-POST improvement**, indicating universal responsiveness to the training stimulus despite temporal differences.

### Upper vs. Lower Body Adaptation Patterns

**When exercises were grouped by body region**, interesting patterns emerged. For 10RM exercises, **both trained and untrained participants showed remarkably similar lower body improvements** (37.9% vs. 37.4%), while **upper body gains favored trained participants** (39.8% vs. 31.1%). **1RM exercises showed more pronounced group differences**, with trained participants demonstrating superior improvements in both upper (32.9% vs. 23.6%) and lower body (39.1% vs. 28.5%) exercises.

**Statistical significance patterns differed between groups and body regions**. Trained participants achieved significant improvements earlier and more consistently, particularly for upper body exercises. **Lower body exercises showed earlier significant improvements in both groups**, suggesting these movements may be more readily adapted to resistance training regardless of aerobic fitness status.

### Temporal Adaptation Characteristics

**The timing of significant improvements varied systematically between exercises and groups**. **Compound lower body exercises** (deadlift, leg press) typically showed significant PRE-MID improvements in both groups, while **upper body exercises** often required the full intervention period to achieve significance, particularly in untrained participants.

**Effect size progression throughout the intervention** demonstrated that most exercises showed increasing percent changes over time rather than diminishing returns, suggesting the 10-week intervention period was appropriate for continued adaptation without plateauing.

### Key Findings Summary

- **Universal improvement**: All participants showed positive percent changes across all exercises
- **Exercise-specific responses**: Deadlift showed the largest improvements (42-45%), bench press the smallest (18-26%)
- **Group differences favor trained**: Particularly pronounced for upper body and technically complex exercises
- **Substantial individual variability**: Maximum improvements ranged from 0% to >130% for individual phases
- **Progressive adaptations**: Most improvements continued throughout the full 10-week period
- **Body region patterns**: Lower body adaptations were more similar between groups than upper body
- **Technical exercise advantage**: Complex movements (overhead press, seated row) favored trained participants

The percent change analysis confirms that both trained and untrained individuals can achieve substantial strength improvements through resistance training, but aerobic fitness may enhance adaptation magnitude and consistency, particularly for upper body and technically demanding exercises. The large individual variability emphasizes the importance of personalized training approaches and monitoring.

```{r Percent Change1, echo=FALSE, results='asis'}
cat(as.character(create_styled_kable(percent_change_summary_with_pvalues,
  caption = "Percent change summary with p-values", digits = 3)))
cat(as.character(create_styled_kable(percent_change_by_upperlower,
  caption = "Percent change by upper and lower exercises", digits = 3)))
cat(as.character(create_styled_kable(percent_change_summary_with_pvalues_TvsU,
  caption = "Percent change summary with p-values by TrainingStatus", digits = 3)))
cat(as.character(create_styled_kable(percent_change_summary_with_pvalues_upperlower_TvsU,
  caption = "Percent change: upper vs lower exercises by TrainingStatus", digits = 3)))
cat(as.character(create_styled_kable(min_max_values,
  caption = "Min and Max values for percent change", digits = 3)))
```



```{r Plotting 10rm, echo=FALSE, warnings=FALSE, message=FALSE}
# Read in the Excel file and perform all transformations in a single pipeline
mydata <- read_excel("MRP.xlsx", sheet = "Strength", col_names = TRUE) %>%
  select(1:3, 7:9) %>%
  rename(Subject = 1, Measure = 2, Exercise = 3, PRE = 4, MID = 5, POST = 6) %>%
  filter(!(Subject %in% c("SUB 5", "SUB 13"))) %>%
  mutate(
    PRE = as.numeric(PRE),
    MID = as.numeric(MID),
    POST = as.numeric(POST)
  )
# Create a new dataframe with 1RM data only, filtering out "Bent Over Row" and removing the Measure column
mydata_1rm <- mydata %>%
  filter(Exercise != "Bent Over Row", str_detect(Measure, "1RM")) %>%
  select(-Measure)
mydata_1rm$Exercise <- dplyr::recode(mydata_1rm$Exercise, !!!replacements)
# Create a new dataframe with 10RM data only, filtering out "Bent Over Row" and removing the Measure column
mydata_10rm <- mydata %>%
  filter(Exercise != "Bent Over Row", str_detect(Measure, "10RM")) %>%
  select(-Measure)
mydata_10rm$Exercise <- dplyr::recode(mydata_10rm$Exercise, !!!replacements)

# Calculate means and standard deviations for each exercise and time point
mydata_10rm_summary <- mydata_10rm %>%
  group_by(Exercise) %>%
  summarise(across(
    c(PRE, MID, POST),
    list(Mean = mean, SD = sd), # Capitalize the Mean here
    .names = "{.col}_{.fn}")  )

# Reshape the data into a longer format
mydata_10rm_summary_long <- mydata_10rm_summary %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols = c(PRE_Mean:POST_SD), # Update column names to match the new pattern
    names_to = c("Time", ".value"),
    names_pattern = "(PRE|MID|POST)_(.*)"
  )

#reorder so that the plot displays in order of magnitude and pre mid post.
mydata_10rm_summary_long <- mydata_10rm_summary_long %>%
  mutate(Exercise = reorder(Exercise, -Mean))
mydata_10rm_summary_long$Time <- factor(mydata_10rm_summary_long$Time, levels = c("PRE", "MID", "POST"))


# create a vector indicating which bars are significantly different
sig_diff_rm10s <- c(F, T, T, F, T, T, F, T, T, F, T, T, F, T, T, F, F, T)

# plot with markers for significant differences COLOR
rm10splot <- ggplot(mydata_10rm_summary_long, aes(x = Exercise, y = Mean, fill = Time)) +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = Mean - SD, ymax = Mean + SD),
                position = position_dodge(width = 0.9), width = 0.2) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity", position = position_dodge(width = 0.9), width = 0.9) +
  geom_text(aes(y = Mean + SD + 2, label = ifelse(sig_diff_rm10s, "*", "")),
            position = position_dodge(width = 0.9), size = 8) +
  labs(x = "", y = "10RM (kg)", fill = "Time") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(dark_blue_color, light_blue_color, third_color)) +  # Apply custom fill colors
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, 165),
                     breaks = seq(0, 160, 20),
                     minor_breaks = seq(0, 160, 10),
                     expand = c(0, 0)) +
  theme_pubr() +
  theme(panel.grid.major = element_blank(),
        panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
        legend.position = c(0.81, 0.85),
        panel.background = element_rect(fill = "transparent", colour = NA),
        panel.border = element_blank(),
        text = element_text(size = 10),          # Set font size for general text
        axis.title = element_text(size = 10, color = "black"),    # Set font size for axis titles
        axis.text = element_text(size = 6, color = "black"),     # Set font size for axis text
        legend.title = element_text(size = 8),  # Set font size for legend title
        legend.text = element_text(size = 8),   # Set font size for legend text))
        legend.key.size = unit(0.3, "cm") )         # Smaller legend keys/symbols


# Save the plot to a png file at 1200 dpi
file_name_plot <- paste0(output_directory, "/", date, "_COLOR_10rm_plot_sig.png")
ggsave(file_name_plot, plot = rm10splot, width = 6, height = 4, dpi = 300)
print(rm10splot)




# Enhanced plotting with ggpubr statistical comparisons and bracket bars
# First, let's prepare the data for ggpubr format

# Convert to long format for ggpubr
prepare_ggpubr_data <- function(data, rm_type) {
  data %>%
    filter(Exercise != "Bent Over Row", str_detect(Measure, rm_type)) %>%
    select(-Measure) %>%
    mutate(Exercise = dplyr::recode(Exercise, !!!replacements)) %>%
    pivot_longer(cols = c(PRE, MID, POST), 
                 names_to = "Time", 
                 values_to = "Weight") %>%
    mutate(Time = factor(Time, levels = c("PRE", "MID", "POST")))
}

# Function to format p-values properly
format_p_value <- function(p) {
  if (is.na(p)) return("ns")
  if (p < 0.001) return(" < 0.001")
  if (p < 0.01) return(paste(sprintf("%.3f", p)))
  if (p < 0.05) return(paste(sprintf("%.3f", p)))
  return("ns")
}

# Create bar plots with individual points, aligned brackets and p-values
create_barplot_with_brackets <- function(data, rm_type, emmeans_results = NULL) {
  
  # Prepare summary data
  summary_data <- data %>%
    group_by(Exercise, Time) %>%
    summarise(
      mean_weight = mean(Weight, na.rm = TRUE),
      se_weight = sd(Weight, na.rm = TRUE) / sqrt(n()),
      .groups = "drop"
    ) %>%
    mutate(Exercise = reorder(Exercise, -mean_weight))
  
  # Reorder the original data to match
  data$Exercise <- factor(data$Exercise, levels = levels(summary_data$Exercise))
  
  # Define consistent bracket heights - lower to overlap with data points
  bracket_heights <- list(
    "PRE-MID" = 130,      # Lowest bracket
    "MID-POST" = 140,     # Middle bracket  
    "PRE-POST" = 150      # Highest bracket
  )
  
  # Create base plot with individual points
  p <- ggplot(summary_data, aes(x = Exercise, y = mean_weight, fill = Time)) +
    # Plot error bars FIRST so they're behind the bars
    geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = mean_weight - se_weight, 
                     ymax = mean_weight + se_weight),
                  position = position_dodge(0.9), 
                  width = 0.2) +
    # Then plot the bars
    geom_col(position = position_dodge(0.9), width = 0.8, alpha = 0.7) +
    # Add individual data points
    geom_point(data = data, aes(x = Exercise, y = Weight, fill = Time),
               position = position_jitterdodge(dodge.width = 0.9, jitter.width = 0.2),
               size = 1.5, alpha = 0.8, shape = 21, stroke = 0.2) +
    scale_fill_manual(values = c(dark_blue_color, light_blue_color, third_color)) +
    labs(x = "", y = paste0(rm_type, " (kg)"), fill = "Time") +
    # Extended scale to match both 1RM and 10RM and accommodate aligned brackets
    scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, 170),  
                       breaks = seq(0, 160, 20),
                       expand = c(0, 0)) +
    theme_pubr() +
    theme(panel.grid.major = element_blank(),
          panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
          legend.position = "bottom",  # Changed for ggarrange
          text = element_text(size = 10),
          axis.title = element_text(size = 10, color = "black"),
          axis.text.x = element_text(size = 8, color = "black", angle = 45, hjust = 1),
          axis.text.y = element_text(size = 8, color = "black"),
          legend.title = element_text(size = 8),
          legend.text = element_text(size = 8),
          legend.key.size = unit(0.4, "cm"))
  
  # Add bracket annotations if we have LMM results
  if (!is.null(emmeans_results)) {
    exercises <- levels(summary_data$Exercise)
    
    for (i in seq_along(exercises)) {
      exercise <- exercises[i]
      measure_name <- paste0(rm_type, "_", exercise)
      
      if (measure_name %in% names(emmeans_results)) {
        timepoint_results <- emmeans_results[[measure_name]]$timepoint
        
        # Get all p-values
        p_pre_mid_idx <- which(timepoint_results$contrast == "PRE - MID")
        p_pre_post_idx <- which(timepoint_results$contrast == "PRE - POST")
        p_mid_post_idx <- which(timepoint_results$contrast == "MID - POST")
        
        # PRE vs MID bracket (if significant)
        if (length(p_pre_mid_idx) > 0) {
          p_pre_mid <- timepoint_results$adj.p.value[p_pre_mid_idx]
          if (!is.na(p_pre_mid) && p_pre_mid < 0.05) {
            bracket_y <- bracket_heights[["PRE-MID"]]
            
            pre_x <- i - 0.3  # PRE bar position
            mid_x <- i         # MID bar position
            
            p_label <- format_p_value(p_pre_mid)
            
            p <- p + 
              annotate("segment", x = pre_x, xend = mid_x, 
                      y = bracket_y, yend = bracket_y, size = 0.5) +
              annotate("segment", x = pre_x, xend = pre_x, 
                      y = bracket_y, yend = bracket_y - 2, size = 0.5) +
              annotate("segment", x = mid_x, xend = mid_x, 
                      y = bracket_y, yend = bracket_y - 2, size = 0.5) +
              annotate("text", x = (pre_x + mid_x)/2, y = bracket_y + 3, 
                      label = p_label, 
                      size = 2.5, hjust = 0.5)
          }
        }
        
        # MID vs POST bracket (if significant)
        if (length(p_mid_post_idx) > 0) {
          p_mid_post <- timepoint_results$adj.p.value[p_mid_post_idx]
          if (!is.na(p_mid_post) && p_mid_post < 0.05) {
            bracket_y <- bracket_heights[["MID-POST"]]
            
            mid_x <- i         # MID bar position
            post_x <- i + 0.3  # POST bar position
            
            p_label <- format_p_value(p_mid_post)
            
            p <- p + 
              annotate("segment", x = mid_x, xend = post_x, 
                      y = bracket_y, yend = bracket_y, size = 0.5) +
              annotate("segment", x = mid_x, xend = mid_x, 
                      y = bracket_y, yend = bracket_y - 2, size = 0.5) +
              annotate("segment", x = post_x, xend = post_x, 
                      y = bracket_y, yend = bracket_y - 2, size = 0.5) +
              annotate("text", x = (mid_x + post_x)/2, y = bracket_y + 3, 
                      label = p_label, 
                      size = 2.5, hjust = 0.5)
          }
        }
        
        # PRE vs POST bracket (if significant) - highest bracket
        if (length(p_pre_post_idx) > 0) {
          p_pre_post <- timepoint_results$adj.p.value[p_pre_post_idx]
          if (!is.na(p_pre_post) && p_pre_post < 0.05) {
            bracket_y <- bracket_heights[["PRE-POST"]]
            
            pre_x <- i - 0.3  # PRE bar position  
            post_x <- i + 0.3  # POST bar position
            
            p_label <- format_p_value(p_pre_post)
            
            p <- p + 
              annotate("segment", x = pre_x, xend = post_x, 
                      y = bracket_y, yend = bracket_y, size = 0.5) +
              annotate("segment", x = pre_x, xend = pre_x, 
                      y = bracket_y, yend = bracket_y - 2, size = 0.5) +
              annotate("segment", x = post_x, xend = post_x, 
                      y = bracket_y, yend = bracket_y - 2, size = 0.5) +
              annotate("text", x = i, y = bracket_y + 3, 
                      label = p_label, 
                      size = 2.5, hjust = 0.5)
          }
        }
      }
    }
  }
  
  return(p)
}

# Prepare data for both 1RM and 10RM
mydata_1rm_long <- prepare_ggpubr_data(mydata, "1RM")
```
